metadata.working_title: Chronic activation of a negative engram induces behavioral and cellular abnormalities

abstract_sketch.problem: Chronic stress and repeated negative thinking are risk factors for mood and memory disorders, but it is unclear if repeated activation of negative memory engrams recapitulates stress-like phenotypes in mice.
abstract_sketch.gap: Whether chronically reactivating a single, tagged negative engram in ventral CA1 over months is sufficient to induce lasting behavioral and cellular abnormalities, and how this depends on age, has not been tested.
abstract_sketch.approach: We used TRAP2 mice to tag a fear-conditioned negative engram in ventral CA1, then chronically activated it with hM3Dq DREADDs and DCZ in drinking water for three months in young (6-month) and aged (14-month) cohorts, assaying anxiety, working memory, fear recall, extinction, and generalization, plus microglia and astrocyte morphology.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Chronic negative-engram activation increased anxiety-like behavior in the open field (Group p<0.0001) and zero maze (Group p<0.0001) at both ages, reduced Y-maze spontaneous alternation in 14-month hM3Dq mice (p=0.0045), impaired fear extinction in 6-month hM3Dq mice (Group p=0.0315), increased generalization freezing at both ages (Group p<0.0001), and altered hippocampal microglia (Iba1+ cells up in 14-month hM3Dq, p=0.0113) and astrocytes (GFAP+ cells up at both ages, 6MO p=0.0328, 14MO p=0.0208), without reducing NeuN+ counts in subiculum, vDG, or vCA1.
abstract_sketch.impact: Chronic activation of a negative engram is sufficient to produce enduring anxiety, cognitive, and glial changes, providing a causal circuit-level model of chronic negative thinking and its age-dependent impact on the hippocampus.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Chronic stress is a risk factor for MDD, GAD, and PTSD; in humans, repeated negative thinking parallels chronic stress effects including hippocampal hyperactivity and accelerated cognitive decline (Marchant et al., 2020).

Prior lab work showed reactivating a positive hippocampal engram produces antidepressant-like effects (Ramirez et al., 2015) and repeated negative reactivation enhances fear (Chen et al., 2019), but the sufficiency of chronic negative-engram activation to drive behavioral and cellular pathology is untested.

Ventral hippocampus (vCA1) modulates affect and the stress response, projecting to BLA, NAc, and PFC (Fanselow and Dong, 2010; Ciocchi et al., 2015); negative engrams in vCA1 influence social avoidance and fear (Chen et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019).

Young and aged brains differ in stress vulnerability: adverse childhood events increase later psychopathology (McLaughlin et al., 2010), while aged rodents show greater stress-induced anxiety, memory impairment, and corticosterone elevation (Shoji and Mizoguchi, 2010; Wang et al., 2020).

Here we chronically reactivate negative engrams in vCA1 of 6- and 14-month mice over three months using TRAP2 x hM3Dq DREADDs and low-dose DCZ drinking water, and assay anxiety, working memory, fear extinction, fear generalization, and glial responses.

subsections.title: Transcriptional signatures distinguish positive and negative memory engrams
content_bullets: Rationale: characterize genetic landscape of positive (male-to-female exposure) and negative (foot shock) engrams to find markers of healthy vs dysfunctional states.
Fluorescence-activated cell sorting of tagged engram cells followed by SMART-Seq v4 Ultra Low Input RNA-seq library preparation.

GSEA (GseaPreranked) on fold-change-ranked protein-coding genes revealed that positive engram cells upregulate anti-inflammatory pathways, while negative engram cells upregulate pro-inflammatory pathways.

subsections.title: Chronic chemogenetic reactivation of a vCA1 negative engram does not disrupt weight gain or neuronal counts
content_bullets: Design: TRAP2 mice received bilateral vCA1 injections of AAV9-DIO-Flex-hM3Dq-mCherry or AAV9-DIO-Flex-mCherry (300 nL total, 150 nL at -4.25 and -4.50 DV), followed by CFC (four 1.5 mA foot shocks) and IP 4-OHT (40 mg/kg) to tag the negative engram.
Chronic activation used DCZ in drinking water (3 ug/kg/day, refreshed every 5 days) for 3 months, taking young (3-month) mice to 6 months and old (11-month) mice to 14 months of age.

Body weight increased normally over three months in both cohorts, with no group effect (6MO Group F(1,17)=1.361, p=0.2594; 14MO Group F(1,26)=0.1275, p=0.7239), showing no gross systemic toxicity (Figure 2C).

No reduction in NeuN+ cell counts in subiculum (Group p=0.9344), vDG (Group p=0.8246), or vCA1 (Group p=0.1244) in hM3Dq vs. mCherry at either age (Supplemental Figure 1), indicating chronic activation did not cause overt neuronal loss.

subsections.title: Chronic negative engram activation increases anxiety-like behavior in both young and aged mice
content_bullets: Open field: 6- and 14-month hM3Dq mice spent less time in the center than age-matched mCherry (Group F(1,33)=29.56, p<0.0001; Tukey 6MO p=0.0049; 14MO p=0.0016) with no difference in total distance (Group p=0.5565) or center entries (Group p=0.5648).
6-month hM3Dq mice had increased average speed in the open field (Group F(1,38)=7.306, p=0.0102; Tukey 6MO p=0.0150; 14MO p=0.9532).

Zero maze: both ages of hM3Dq mice spent less time in the open arms (Group F(1,38)=40.22, p<0.0001; Tukey 6MO p=0.0002; 14MO p=0.0007), with no differences in total distance, open-area entries, or average speed.

subsections.title: Chronic negative engram activation impairs spatial working memory in aged mice
content_bullets: Y-maze: 14-month hM3Dq mice showed reduced spontaneous alternation vs. age-matched mCherry (Interaction F(1,40)=4.088, p=0.0499; Group F(1,40)=7.517, p=0.0091; Tukey 14MO p=0.0045).
No difference between 6-month mCherry and hM3Dq (Tukey p=0.9634), indicating age-dependent working-memory vulnerability.
No significant differences in total distance traveled (Group p=0.2831) or total arm entries (Group p=0.3692).

subsections.title: Chronic negative engram activation impairs fear extinction in young mice and increases generalization at both ages
content_bullets: CFC acquisition: no group differences in freezing during the 300 s CFC session (6MO Group F(1,26)=2.737, p=0.1101; 14MO Group F(1,18)=1.862, p=0.1892).

Remote recall (3 months post-CFC, context A): 6-month hM3Dq mice froze more than 6-month mCherry (Group F(1,17)=6.588, p=0.0200); this remote-recall group effect was absent in 14-month cohorts (Group p=0.5343), while total freezing increased with age in mCherry (6 vs 14MO mCherry p=0.0101).
Extinction (30 min in context A): 6-month hM3Dq mice maintained higher freezing vs. mCherry (Group F(1,17)=5.494, p=0.0315); no group effect in 14-month cohorts (Group p=0.5411).

Generalization (novel context B): both ages of hM3Dq mice froze more than age-matched mCherry (Group F(1,41)=26.53, p<0.0001; Tukey 6MO p=0.0053; 14MO p=0.0026), indicating broadened fear across contexts.

subsections.title: Chronic negative engram activation remodels hippocampal microglia and astrocytes
content_bullets: Microglia (Iba1+) counts increased only in 14-month hM3Dq (Interaction F(1,8)=7.615, p=0.0247; Group F(1,8)=13.42, p=0.0064; Tukey 14MO p=0.0113; 6MO p=0.9906).

14-month hM3Dq microglia had increased ramification index (Group p=0.0046; Tukey 14MO p=0.03242), cell territory volume (Group p=0.0037; 14MO p=0.0129), minimum branch length (Group p=0.0257; 14MO p=0.0174) and maximum branch length (Group p=0.0172; 14MO p=0.0309).
Astrocyte (GFAP+) counts increased in hM3Dq at both ages (Group F(1,8)=26.95, p=0.0008; Tukey 6MO p=0.0328; 14MO p=0.0208).

Astrocyte average branch length (Group p=0.0004) and maximum branch length (Group p=0.0002) increased in hM3Dq at both ages; minimum branch length increased in 6-month hM3Dq only (Group p=0.0038; Tukey 6MO p=0.0238; 14MO p=0.2759).
n=3 mice/group x 18 single-tile ROIs per region for cell counts, and 3 mice/group x 3 ROIs (20-30 cells each) for morphology.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Summary: three months of chronic vCA1 negative-engram activation is sufficient to induce anxiety, generalization, age-dependent working-memory and extinction deficits, and glial remodeling.

Mechanistic interpretation: positive vs negative engrams upregulate anti- vs pro-inflammatory pathways respectively, suggesting a molecular substrate by which chronic negative reactivation could drive glial activation.

Age-dependent vulnerabilities: older mice show microglial expansion and working-memory deficits; younger mice show impaired extinction and increased remote recall, consistent with known age-by-stress interactions (Shoji and Mizoguchi, 2010; Wang et al., 2020).

Comparison to chronic stress models: phenotypes parallel those in chronic social defeat, chronic unpredictable stress, and repeated negative-rumination paradigms in humans, supporting a memory-reactivation component of rumination-like states (Marchant et al., 2020).

Limitations: TRAP2 vs DOX tagging captures different fractions of CA1 pyramidal cells (5-18% vs 20-40%; DeNardo et al., 2019; Monasterio et al., 2024); DCZ pharmacokinetics peak 5-10 min after IP (Nagai et al., 2020) and our drinking-water route delivers constant low-dose; DREADD toxicity risk at high AAV titers (Goossens et al., 2021); E/I balance not yet resolved.
Future work: excitatory and inhibitory marker quantification, longitudinal imaging of glia, and projection-specific targeting of vCA1 outputs to BLA, NAc, and PFC.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Animals: TRAP2 mice on C57BL/6J background, young (3 mo at surgery) and old (11 mo at surgery) cohorts, IACUC protocol 201800579 Boston University.
Stereotaxic surgery: bilateral vCA1 injection of 300 nL AAV9-DIO-Flex-hM3Dq-mCherry or AAV9-DIO-Flex-mCherry at -3.16 AP, +/- 3.10 ML, -4.25/-4.50 DV; 4 week recovery.
DCZ dosing: 3 ug/kg/day via home-cage water, refreshed every 5 days (HelloBio HB9126), approx 6 mL water/day.
CFC tagging: four 1.5 mA foot shocks in context A; 40 mg/kg IP 4-OHT 30 min post-CFC.

Behavioral battery: open field (61x61 cm, 10 min), zero maze (44 cm wide ring, 62 cm outer diameter), Y-maze (36.5 x 7.5 x 12.5 cm arms), remote recall (5 min in context A), extinction (30 min in context A), generalization (context B, larger chamber).
Histology: 50 um coronal/sagittal sections, Iba1 and GFAP z-stacks at 1 um, NeuN z-stacks at 4 um; 3DMorph MATLAB toolbox for glial morphology (Supplemental Figure 2).
Statistics: GraphPad Prism v9.4.1; two-way (RM) ANOVA with Sidak's or Tukey's post-hoc; ROUT Q=10% for outliers; n=9-17 per group for behavior, n=3 mice/group for histology.
